texts.title: New Testament
texts.author: Various Apostolic Authors
texts.notes: P52 (John 18:31–33) dated ~100–150 AD is the earliest fragment. Over 5,800 Greek manuscripts alone, plus 20,000+ in other languages.

texts.title: Julius Caesar — Gallic Wars
texts.author: Julius Caesar
texts.notes: We rely on 10 manuscripts in good condition, the earliest from ~900 AD. No historian doubts Caesar wrote it.

texts.title: Homer — The Iliad
texts.author: Homer
texts.notes: Highest manuscript count of any ancient secular text — which is why the Iliad is considered exceptionally well-attested. The NT has 14x more manuscripts.

texts.title: Plato — Dialogues
texts.author: Plato
texts.notes: The earliest complete manuscript of Plato's dialogues is from ~895 AD — over 1,200 years after Plato wrote. No serious historian doubts their authenticity.

texts.title: Aristotle — Works
texts.author: Aristotle
texts.notes: We have only 49 manuscripts of Aristotle, the earliest from ~1100 AD — 1,450 years after the originals. Aristotle's authorship is universally accepted.

texts.title: Tacitus — Annals
texts.author: Tacitus
texts.notes: Only 33 manuscripts survive. Tacitus is one of the primary ancient sources for the reign of Tiberius — accepted without question by historians.

texts.title: Thucydides — History of the Peloponnesian War
texts.author: Thucydides
texts.notes: 96 manuscripts, earliest from ~900 AD. The foundational text of Western historical writing — its authenticity is never questioned.

texts.title: Herodotus — Histories
texts.author: Herodotus
texts.notes: 109 manuscripts surviving. Called the 'Father of History.' His accounts are accepted by historians with far fewer manuscripts than the NT.

key_insight: The consistency principle: every ancient text we accept as authentic has fewer manuscripts, larger time gaps, and less corroborating evidence than the New Testament. If you accept Caesar, Homer, or Plato — intellectual consistency requires taking the NT manuscript evidence at least as seriously.
footnotes: Manuscript counts are approximate and vary by source. NT count of 25,000 includes Greek, Latin, Syriac, Coptic, Armenian, and other language manuscripts.
Sources: F.F. Bruce, 'The New Testament Documents: Are They Reliable?'; Norman Geisler & William Nix, 'A General Introduction to the Bible'; Bruce Metzger, 'The Text of the New Testament.'
